nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef flats, reef slopes, and lagoons throughout the tropical Indo-Pacific; typically found in areas with moderate to good light penetration

decorPreferences: adequate spacing from neighboring corals (15+ cm)

diet.notes: Like most reef corals, Lobophytum is primarily photosynthetic, drawing the majority of its nutrition from symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae) hosted within its tissue — meaning good lighting is more important to its long-term health than regular target feeding. The coral tends to be less efficient at capturing food particles directly than many other coral types, though it does absorb dissolved nutrients and fine particulates from the water column. Supplemental offerings of micro-plankton, baby brine shrimp, or invertebrate-targeted liquid foods are sometimes provided by hobbyists, though most sources indicate this is not strictly necessary in a well-lit system. Regular additions of iodine, strontium, and trace elements are commonly recommended to support growth and tissue health.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals with adequate spacing
other soft corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: SPS corals (Acropora, Montipora, etc.) in close proximity — allelopathic terpenoid release
Sarcophyton species without substantial buffer space
Sinularia species without substantial buffer space

summary: Lobophytum Coral — sold under names like Devil's Hand, Flat Leather, and Finger Leather Coral — is a large-bodied soft coral from the tropical Indo-Pacific. Generally considered one of the more forgiving corals for beginners, it has been documented releasing chemical compounds that can inhibit nearby stony corals, making activated carbon helpful in mixed-reef tanks.
careNotes:
Note: Lobophytum is a large genus of roughly 67–70+ recognized species. In the aquarium trade, specimens are almost universally sold at genus level (Lobophytum sp.) because distinguishing species requires microscopic examination of the tiny calcium carbonate structures (sclerites) embedded in the coral's tissue — a level of analysis not practical at retail. All species share near-identical husbandry requirements and are generally kept and cared for the same way.

Lobophytum is closely related to Sarcophyton and Sinularia within the family Alcyoniidae. It is distinguished by its dimorphic polyps: a set of larger, prominent feeding polyps and a layer of very small polyps embedded in the capitulum (the flat or lobed top surface of the colony). Colonies tend to grow as flat to lobed mats with finger-like projections from the edges and surface, giving rise to common names like Devil's Hand and Flat Leather Coral.

Key care points:
• Lighting: Moderate lighting is generally advised — approximately 75–200 PAR. Lobophytum tends to adapt to a range of intensities, making it forgiving for hobbyists with varied lighting setups. Avoid sudden large changes in light intensity, which can trigger stress responses.
• Flow: Moderate, intermittent water movement is commonly recommended. Polyps should visibly sway. Avoid strong direct laminar flow aimed at the base of the colony.
• Placement: Middle of the aquarium is generally advised to achieve moderate light levels. Allow at least 15 cm of buffer space between Lobophytum and any neighboring corals — particularly SPS corals and other Alcyoniidae — to reduce allelopathic pressure.
• Chemical allelopathy: Lobophytum has been documented producing terpenoids and other secondary metabolites that can inhibit nearby stony corals (SPS) at a reported influence range of roughly 10–15 cm. Running activated carbon continuously is widely recommended in mixed reef systems containing this coral. Regular partial water changes further dilute accumulated compounds.
• Waxy shedding: A translucent waxy or mucus coating that periodically sheds from the surface is normal behavior. The colony may appear shriveled or contracted for several days during and after the shed cycle before re-expanding.
• Water quality: Unlike SPS corals, Lobophytum has been reported to tolerate moderate fluctuations in water parameters and higher nutrient levels. Nitrate below 20 ppm and phosphate below 0.1 ppm are generally sufficient, though lower is preferred for long-term health.
• Colony size: In the wild, colonies can grow to 40 cm or more across. In home aquaria, colonies typically reach 10–30 cm depending on available space and conditions. Plan for lateral spread over time.
• Fragmentation: Lobophytum responds well to fragging. Cuttings can be made with clean scissors and attached to rock rubble or frag plugs.
• Tank age: Best kept in an established reef system (at least 3–6 months of cycling); not suitable for brand-new tanks.
breedingNotes: Asexual propagation via fragmentation is widely practiced and generally straightforward. Clean cuts can be made with scissors or a razor, and the frag attached to live rock rubble or a frag plug with a rubber band or reef-safe adhesive until it encrusts. Sexual reproduction via broadcast spawning occurs on natural reefs during coordinated mass spawning events but is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Devil's Hand Leather Coral
primary.notes: Listed as Lobophytum sp. Provided: care level Easy, temp 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12, lighting Moderate to High, flow Medium to Strong, placement All, temperament Peaceful. Notes supplemental feeding beneficial; requires iodine, strontium, trace elements.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Lobophytum (taxonomy and distribution)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family: Alcyoniidae, Order: Malacalcyonacea, Class: Octocorallia) and native range confirmation (shallow tropical Indo-Pacific). Confirmed ~67–70 species and common name devil's hand coral. Not used for husbandry parameters.

additional.name: ReefBuilders – Get to know the leather corals: Sarcophyton, Lobophytum and Sinularia
additional.notes: Confirmed dimorphic polyp morphology, medium-to-high light and flow requirements, photosynthetic nutrition with poor direct-feeding capacity, space requirements between colonies, and the normal waxy shedding behavior. Noted these corals are more tolerant of parameter fluctuations than SPS/LPS.

additional.name: LiveAquaria – Green Spaghetti Finger Leather Coral (Sinularia brassica cross-check)
additional.notes: Cross-reference for sister leather-coral genus (Sinularia). Confirmed identical water parameter targets: temp 72–78°F, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12. Also documented allelopathic compounds harmful to stony corals and recommendation for activated carbon and routine water changes. Semi-aggressive temperament and medium-to-strong flow confirmed for related genus.
